title: President (corporate title)

infobox.value: President
[Employment](/wiki/Employment "Employment")
[Business](/wiki/Business "Business")
Leadership, financial skills

[CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer"), [executive officer](/wiki/Executive_officer "Executive officer"), [vice president](/wiki/Vice_president "Vice president"), managing director, representative director, [COO](/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer"), [general manager](/wiki/General_manager "General manager"), [chairman](/wiki/Chairman "Chairman"), vice-chairman

is a leader of an [organization](/wiki/Organization "Organization"), [company](/wiki/Company "Company"), community, club, [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union"), [university](/wiki/University "University"), country or other group.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The relationship between a president and a [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") varies, depending on the structure of the specific organization. In a similar vein to a [chief operating officer](/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer"), the [title](/wiki/Title "Title") of corporate president as a separate [position](/wiki/Job_(role) "Job (role)") (as opposed to being combined with a "[C-suite](/wiki/Corporate_title#Senior_management "Corporate title")" designation, such as "president and chief executive officer" or "president and chief operating officer") is also loosely [defined](/wiki/Definition "Definition"); the president is usually the legally [recognized](/wiki/Legal_recognition "Legal recognition") highest [rank](/wiki/Rank "Rank") of [corporate officer](/wiki/Corporate_title "Corporate title"), ranking above the various [vice presidents](/wiki/Vice_president#In_business "Vice president") (including senior vice president and executive vice president), but on its own generally considered [subordinate](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy"), in practice, to the CEO. The powers of a president vary widely across organizations and such powers come from specific authorization in the [bylaws](/wiki/By-law "By-law") like *[Robert's Rules of Order](/wiki/Robert%27s_Rules_of_Order "Robert's Rules of Order")* (e.g. the president can make an "executive decision" only if the bylaws allow for it).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

["Example of Presidential Jobs"](https://web.archive.org/web/20190407224350/https://schoolofunityandconnectness.teachable.com/p/faqs). Archived from [the original](https://schoolofunityandconnectness.teachable.com/p/faqs) on April 7, 2019.

["Example of President and Leaders"](https://web.archive.org/web/20190413000936/https://schoolofunityandconnectness.teachable.com/p/administrating). Archived from [the original](https://schoolofunityandconnectness.teachable.com/p/administrating) on April 13, 2019.

Robert, Henry M.; et al. (2011). *[Robert's Rules of Order](/wiki/Robert%27s_Rules_of_Order "Robert's Rules of Order") Newly Revised* (11th ed.). Philadelphia, PA: Da Capo Press. p. 456. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-306-82020-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-306-82020-5 "Special:BookSources/978-0-306-82020-5").
Raz, Guy (December 14, 2013). ["'President' Once Meant Little More Than 'Foreman'"](https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=250990126). *npr.org*. Retrieved December 12, 2012.
["President"](http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/president?s=t). *Dictionary.com*. Retrieved December 19, 2015. "a person who presides."
[Robert 2011](#CITEREFRobert2011), p. 456
[Robert 2011](#CITEREFRobert2011), p. 449
[Robert 2011](#CITEREFRobert2011), p. 44: "The presiding officer must never interrupt a speaker simply because he knows more about the matter than the speaker does."

["Frequently Asked Questions about RONR (Question 1)"](https://web.archive.org/web/20151207130520/http://www.robertsrules.com/faq.html#1). *The Official Robert's Rules of Order Web Site*. The Robert's Rules Association. Archived from [the original](http://www.robertsrules.com/faq.html#1) on December 7, 2015. Retrieved December 19, 2015.
[Robert 2011](#CITEREFRobert2011), p. 406

["Frequently Asked Questions about RONR (Question 20)"](https://web.archive.org/web/20151207130520/http://www.robertsrules.com/faq.html#20). *The Official Robert's Rules of Order Web Site*. The Robert's Rules Association. Archived from [the original](http://www.robertsrules.com/faq.html#20) on December 7, 2015. Retrieved December 24, 2015.
[Robert 2011](#CITEREFRobert2011), p. 457

Kubilus, Norbert (April 29, 2013). ["What is the Immediate Past President?"](https://web.archive.org/web/20150906142938/https://www.aitp.org/news/123604/). *[Association of Information Technology Professionals](/wiki/Association_of_Information_Technology_Professionals "Association of Information Technology Professionals")*. Archived from [the original](https://www.aitp.org/news/123604/) on September 6, 2015. Retrieved January 30, 2016.

Archived from [the original](http://www.asha.org/About/governance/BOD/President/) on February 4, 2016. Retrieved January 30, 2016.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Leader of an organization

President

| Occupation | |
| --- | --- |
| Names | President |
| Occupation type | [Employment](/wiki/Employment "Employment") |
| Activity sectors | [Business](/wiki/Business "Business") |
| Description | |
| Competencies | Leadership, financial skills |
| Related jobs | [CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer"), [executive officer](/wiki/Executive_officer "Executive officer"), [vice president](/wiki/Vice_president "Vice president"), managing director, representative director, [COO](/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer"), [general manager](/wiki/General_manager "General manager"), [chairman](/wiki/Chairman "Chairman"), vice-chairman |

A **president** is a leader of an [organization](/wiki/Organization "Organization"), [company](/wiki/Company "Company"), community, club, [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union"), [university](/wiki/University "University"), country or other group.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The relationship between a president and a [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") varies, depending on the structure of the specific organization. In a similar vein to a [chief operating officer](/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer"), the [title](/wiki/Title "Title") of corporate president as a separate [position](/wiki/Job_(role) "Job (role)") (as opposed to being combined with a "[C-suite](/wiki/Corporate_title#Senior_management "Corporate title")" designation, such as "president and chief executive officer" or "president and chief operating officer") is also loosely [defined](/wiki/Definition "Definition"); the president is usually the legally [recognized](/wiki/Legal_recognition "Legal recognition") highest [rank](/wiki/Rank "Rank") of [corporate officer](/wiki/Corporate_title "Corporate title"), ranking above the various [vice presidents](/wiki/Vice_president#In_business "Vice president") (including senior vice president and executive vice president), but on its own generally considered [subordinate](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy"), in practice, to the CEO. The powers of a president vary widely across organizations and such powers come from specific authorization in the [bylaws](/wiki/By-law "By-law") like *[Robert's Rules of Order](/wiki/Robert%27s_Rules_of_Order "Robert's Rules of Order")* (e.g. the president can make an "executive decision" only if the bylaws allow for it).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=President_(corporate_title)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Originally, the term *president* was used in the same way that *foreman* or *overseer* is used now (the term is still used in that sense today).[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) It has now also come to mean "chief officer" in terms of administrative or executive duties.

## Powers and authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=President_(corporate_title)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Powers and authority")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/AGM_Annual_General_Meeting_of_a_typical_small_%28141_member%29_volunteer_organisation.jpg/250px-AGM_Annual_General_Meeting_of_a_typical_small_%28141_member%29_volunteer_organisation.jpg)](/wiki/File:AGM_Annual_General_Meeting_of_a_typical_small_(141_member)_volunteer_organisation.jpg)

President presiding over the AGM of a small volunteer organization. President sitting at the left of table in the background.

The powers of the president vary widely across organizations. In some organizations the president has the authority to hire staff and make financial decisions, while in others the president only makes recommendations to a [board of directors](/wiki/Board_of_directors "Board of directors"), and still others the president has no executive powers and is mainly a [spokesperson](/wiki/Spokesperson "Spokesperson") for the [organization](/wiki/Organization "Organization"). The amount of power given to the president depends on the type of organization, its structure, and the rules it has created for itself.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In addition to administrative or executive duties in organizations, a president has the duties of presiding over meetings.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Such duties at meetings include:

* calling the meeting to order
* determining if a [quorum](/wiki/Quorum "Quorum") is present
* announcing the items on the [order of business](/wiki/Order_of_business "Order of business") or [agenda](/wiki/Agenda_(meeting) "Agenda (meeting)") as they come up
* [recognition](/wiki/Recognition_(parliamentary_procedure) "Recognition (parliamentary procedure)") of members to have the [floor](/wiki/Floor_(legislative) "Floor (legislative)")
* enforcing the rules of the group
* putting all questions ([motions](/wiki/Motion_(parliamentary_procedure) "Motion (parliamentary procedure)")) to a vote
* adjourning the meeting

While presiding, a president remains impartial and does not interrupt speakers if a speaker has the floor and is following the rules of the group.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In committees or small boards, the president votes along with the other members. However, in [assemblies](/wiki/General_assembly "General assembly") or larger boards, the president should vote only when it can affect the result.[[9]](#cite_note-9) At a meeting, the president only has one vote (i.e. the president cannot vote twice and cannot override the decision of the group unless the organisation has specifically given the president such authority).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Disciplinary procedures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=President_(corporate_title)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Disciplinary procedures")]

If the president exceeds the given authority, engages in misconduct, or fails to perform the duties, the president may face disciplinary procedures. Such procedures may include [censure](/wiki/Censure "Censure"), suspension, or [removal from office](/wiki/Declare_the_chair_vacant "Declare the chair vacant"). The rules of the particular organization would provide details on who can perform these disciplinary procedures and the extent that they can be done.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Usually, whoever appointed or elected the president has the power to discipline this officer.

## President-elect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=President_(corporate_title)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: President-elect")]

Some organizations may have a position of [president-elect](/wiki/President-elect "President-elect") in addition to the position of president. Generally the membership of the organization elects a president-elect and when the term of the outgoing president is complete, the president-elect automatically becomes president.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Immediate past president

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=President_(corporate_title)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Immediate past president")]

Some organizations may have a position of immediate past president in addition to the position of president.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) In those organizations, when the term of the president is complete, that person automatically fills the position of immediate past president. The organization can have such a position only if the bylaws provide it.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-16) The duties of such a position would also have to be provided in the bylaws.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-16)

## Life president

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=President_(corporate_title)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Life president")]

Life president is an [honorary](/wiki/Title_of_honor "Title of honor") title often given to someone who has already served the organization for a long period in a major role.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=President_(corporate_title)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Poland")]

In [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), due to the fact that there is a "[civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)")" system instead of "[common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law")", the equivalent of "[CEO](/wiki/CEO "CEO")" is "President of the Management Board".[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)
